title: César Aira’s Art of Not Editing
description: “A bird, for example, flew into Aira’s café while he was writing, and he worked it into the story.”
hook: Aira writes novels in cafés where birds fly through windows and immediately become plot points—he has no system, only total surrender to whatever arrives.

title: What Sally Rooney’s Fiction Illuminates About Real-Life Marriage
description:
Watching Normal People by Katy Whitehead They started watching Normal People, like everybody else, during the first lockdown. They’d been married three years at this point. She’d read two of Rooney’s novels, and both of her published stories (as well as much of the criticism) so she knew it wasn’t going to be socialist in […]

The post What Sally Rooney’s Fiction Illumin
hook: Sally Rooney's prose about marriage isn't romantic—it's a document of two people learning what it costs to be fully known by another person.

title: Elves Against Capitalism: How the Earth Liberation Front Came to America
description: The fires began with a gentle Midwestern boy who loved animals. Kevin Tubbs grew up in a comfortable suburb outside Omaha, Nebraska, in a canary-yellow ranch house filled with dogs. Tubbs’s mother, a tender-hearted woman given to long spells of
hook: How a gentle Midwestern boy who loved animals became part of the Earth Liberation Front—a portrait of radicalization disguised as environmental activism.

title: Louis C.K. Is Stuck in the Past
description: The comedian’s new special, Ridiculous, lacks the honesty of his best work.
hook: Louis C.K.'s new special retreads old material: the comedian once mined shame for insight; now he's just defending himself.

title: ‘The Listeners’: Rebecca Hall and Janicza Bravo Break Down Show’s Ending and Why That Finale Death Was So ‘Heartbreaking’
description: The limited series' director and star go deep with IndieWire about their creative love affair, the characters who needed matching haircuts, and what to make of a wrenching finale to this on-screen adaptation of Jordan Tannahill's novel.
hook: The ending of 'The Listeners' required Rebecca Hall and Janicza Bravo to kill a character they loved—they explain why that death had to happen.
